test-requirement.description: Production volumes cannot be negative. Pressure and temperature cannot be negative. Water cut must be between 0 and 1. GOR (gas-oil ratio) must be non-negative. These are physical laws, not data quality preferences — any violation is either a sensor error or a pipeline bug and should be caught explicitly.

Oil volumes should be in BBL, gas in MCF, water in BBL throughout. If the raw data mixes units across wells or time periods (common in KGS data), the pipeline should normalize them and a test should verify the normalized values fall within realistic ranges — for example oil rates above 50,000 BBL/month for a single well are almost certainly a unit error.

Cumulative production (Np, Gp, Wp) should be monotonically non-decreasing over time per well. A well cannot un-produce oil. If cumulative values decrease between months, the pipeline has either sorted incorrectly or introduced a processing error.

Each well in the processed folder should have a continuous date range with no unexpected gaps. A well producing from January to December should have 12 monthly records, not 11. Test that the count of records per well matches the expected span from first to last production date.

Wells that reported zero production in a given month are different from wells with missing data for that month. Your pipeline should preserve the distinction — a zero is a valid measurement, a null is missing data. Test that zeros in the raw data remain as zeros (not nulls) in the processed data.

Gas-Oil Ratio (GOR) is defined as gas_mcf / oil_bbl. When oil_bbl is zero and gas_mcf is greater than zero — a physically valid state for a late-life or pure gas well — the result is mathematically undefined. The pipeline must not raise a ZeroDivisionError or produce a NaN silently swallowed by downstream operations. Test explicitly that: (a) oil_bbl=0 and gas_mcf>0 returns NaN or a defined sentinel value, not an exception; (b) both oil_bbl=0 and gas_mcf=0 (shut-in well) returns 0 or NaN, not an exception; (c) oil_bbl>0 and gas_mcf=0 returns 0.0, not NaN. Do not treat high GOR on low oil production as a data error — a well in pressure depletion below bubble point will produce predominantly gas with very little oil, yielding a legitimately high GOR.

The pipeline computes period-over-period production decline rate as a feature and clips it to the range [-1.0, 10.0]. These are engineering design bounds for ML feature stability, not physical constants. Test that: (a) a computed decline rate below -1.0 is clipped to exactly -1.0; (b) a computed decline rate above 10.0 is clipped to exactly 10.0; (c) a value within bounds passes through unchanged; (d) a well with zero production in two consecutive months (shut-in) does not produce an unclipped extreme value before clipping is applied — the raw computation should be handled before the clip is evaluated. Use synthetic well sequences with known month-over-month changes to make assertions exact.

A well that is temporarily shut in will report zero production for one or more months. Cumulative production (Np for oil, Gp for gas, Wp for water) must remain flat — equal to the previous month's value — not decrease, and not skip forward incorrectly. This is a separate failure mode from the monotonicity decrease case: a recalculation bug can produce a flat period where cumulative jumps to a wrong value when production resumes. Test with a synthetic sequence containing: (a) one or more consecutive zero-production months mid-sequence; (b) zero-production months at the start of the record (well not yet online); (c) resumption of production after shut-in, verifying cumulative resumes correctly from the flat value.

The features stage computes derived columns including rolling averages, lag features, GOR, and water cut. These must be verified for correctness against a known synthetic input, not merely for the absence of errors. Required checks: (a) rolling 3-month and 6-month oil, gas, and water averages match hand-computed values for a known sequence; (b) when the rolling window is larger than the available history (e.g. first 2 months of a well's life), the result is NaN or a partial window as specified — not silently zero or a wrong value; (c) lag-1 feature for month N equals the raw production value for month N-1, verified for at least 3 consecutive months; (d) GOR formula is gas_mcf / oil_bbl with correct unit handling — not gas_bbl / oil_mcf or any unit-swapped variant; (e) water cut formula is water_bbl / (oil_bbl + water_bbl) — denominator uses total liquid, not just oil.

test-requirement.n: Water cut boundary values — valid at 0 and 1
test-requirement.description: Water cut of exactly 0.0 is physically valid — a new well or a well in a dry reservoir produces no water. Water cut of exactly 1.0 is also physically valid — a well in late-life producing 100% water with trace or zero oil. Neither value should be treated as an outlier, flagged, or nulled by the cleaning or feature engineering stage. Test that: (a) a row with water_bbl=0 and oil_bbl>0 produces water_cut=0.0 and is retained in the processed output; (b) a row with oil_bbl=0 and water_bbl>0 produces water_cut=1.0 and is retained — this is a valid end-of-life well state, not a data error; (c) only values outside [0, 1] are treated as invalid.
Spot-checking production volumes from raw to processed for randomly chosen wells and months run across a statistically meaningful sample.
Checks to confirm appropriate handling of nulls, missing values, outliers and duplicates. Checks to see if data-types have been set correctly in the processed data files (datetime, float, str).
Verify that each Parquet partition file contains data for exactly one well — no partition should contain rows from multiple well_id values.

All processed Parquet files across all wells should have identical column names and data types. It is possible for per-well processing to produce schema drift if one well has all nulls in a column and type inference guesses wrong. Test that a schema sampled from one well's file matches a schema sampled from another.

After deduplication, the processed row count should be less than or equal to the raw row count, never greater. Also test that the deduplication is idempotent — running the cleaning step twice on the same data should produce the same output as running it once.

Verify that the sort is stable across the partition boundary — the last row of one Parquet file for a well should have a date earlier than the first row of the next file for the same well, if the data is partitioned into multiple files per well.

Verify that the pipeline modules do not call .compute() internally. This can be tested by checking that the return type of each pipeline function is dask.dataframe.DataFrame rather than pandas.DataFrame. A function returning a pandas DataFrame has either called .compute() or bypassed Dask entirely.

Test that every output Parquet file is actually readable by a fresh Dask or pandas process. A file that was written incorrectly can exist on disk but raise an error on read. This catches silent write failures.

test-requirement.n: Feature column presence in output schema
test-requirement.description: The features Parquet output must contain all expected derived columns. A run that silently drops a feature column due to a computation error or an unhandled exception that is caught and swallowed will pass schema stability and partition tests but produce unusable ML output. Test that the output DataFrame contains all of the following columns: well_id, production_date, oil_bbl, gas_mcf, water_bbl, cum_oil, cum_gas, cum_water, gor, water_cut, decline_rate, and all rolling and lag columns specified in the features task spec. Use a synthetic single-well input and assert on the complete column list.

Running the acquire stage twice on the same target directory must not duplicate files, corrupt existing files, or raise an error because a file already exists. The acquire step should detect already-downloaded files and skip them. Test that: (a) after a first acquire run, a second run on the same directory produces the same file count — not doubled; (b) the content of a pre-existing file is unchanged after the second run; (c) no exception is raised when the target file already exists on disk.

A file that was downloaded but is empty (0 bytes) or is not valid UTF-8 text is a silent failure — it will exist on disk and pass a file-exists check but cause an error at ingest time. Test that: (a) every file in the raw data directory after acquire has size greater than 0 bytes; (b) every file is readable as text without a UnicodeDecodeError; (c) every file contains at least one row of data beyond a header line. Use the output of a real or mocked acquire run as the test fixture.

After ingest is complete, read a sample of at least 3 interim Parquet partitions and assert that all expected columns are present in every partition: LEASE_KID, LEASE, API_NUMBER, MONTH_YEAR, PRODUCT, PRODUCTION, WELLS, OPERATOR, COUNTY, source_file. Assert no expected column is missing or renamed in any sampled partition.
